terms.def: The gap between the market maker's buy price (bid) and sell price (ask); the market maker's gross revenue per round-trip.
Risk of holding a large directional position while making markets; price moves against accumulated inventory.
Informed traders take from market makers at unfair prices; MM widens spreads to protect against informed flow.
Adjusting bid/ask asymmetrically based on inventory to encourage trades that reduce risk.
High-Frequency Trading — market making at microsecond speed using co-located servers and ultra-low latency.
% of quoted orders that are filled by incoming trades; too high = adverse selection, too low = missing volume.

eli3: A market maker is a firm or individual that continuously quotes both a price to buy (bid) and a price to sell (ask) for a security, making it easy for others to trade. The market maker profits from the bid-ask spread — buying at the lower bid price and selling at the higher ask price.

Without market makers, buying or selling would require finding someone who wants to do the opposite trade at the exact same time. Market makers solve this coordination problem by being always ready to take the other side of any trade, providing 'liquidity' to the market.

Modern market making is highly automated. HFT firms use ultra-fast algorithms to quote prices in thousands of securities simultaneously, managing their inventory and hedging risk in real time. The profit margins per trade are tiny (fractions of a cent) but the volume is enormous.

whenWhy.title: Providing continuous liquidity and enabling price discovery
whenWhy.body: Market makers are the infrastructure of financial markets. Without them, markets would be 'quote-driven' only when natural buyers and sellers arrive simultaneously — illiquid and slow. Market makers stand ready to buy when sellers arrive and sell when buyers arrive, ensuring trades can execute at any time. Their continuous quoting provides pre-trade price transparency (the bid/ask prices). The spread they charge compensates for: inventory risk (holding unwanted positions), adverse selection (trading against better-informed counterparties), and operational costs. Without this compensation, no rational agent would provide continuous liquidity.

whenWhy.title: Earning the bid-ask spread while managing inventory risk
whenWhy.body: The market maker's P&L: earn the spread (sell at ask, buy at bid) × turnover, minus losses from inventory positions that move against them. Inventory accumulates when one side dominates (net selling pressure → long inventory → long delta risk). Market makers manage inventory by: skewing quotes (lower bid AND ask when long, to attract more selling and discourage buying), using futures/ETF hedges to offset delta, and temporarily widening spreads to reduce activity when inventory is uncomfortable. The goal: zero inventory at the end of the day, having collected the spread on all trades.

whenWhy.title: Earning adverse selection compensation through speed and signal detection
whenWhy.body: Adverse selection is the primary risk for market makers: informed traders (who know the stock will move) transact against them at the current quotes, then the price moves against the market maker's position. HFT market makers manage this by: (1) detecting informed order flow from signals (correlated market moves, order book imbalance, recent trade direction) and widening spreads or cancelling quotes before the informed trader can act; (2) using co-location (placing servers at the exchange) to detect and respond to price changes in microseconds; (3) only quoting liquid instruments where the order flow is less information-driven. The ability to detect and respond to adverse selection faster than counterparties is the HFT market maker's core competitive advantage.

deepDiveIntro: The Avellaneda-Stoikov (2008) model provides the theoretical foundation for optimal market making. It models the mid-price as a random walk and derives the bid and ask quotes that maximize expected terminal wealth minus an inventory penalty. The key insight is a reservation price — the mid-price adjusted downward when the market maker holds a long inventory, reflecting the cost of carrying that position. The optimal spread widens when inventory is large, time to end of day is short, or price volatility is high. In practice, HFT market makers build on this framework by combining it with real-time signals such as order book imbalance and correlated asset prices, updating quotes in microseconds.

deepDive.heading: Avellaneda-Stoikov Optimal Market Making
deepDive.body: The mid-price follows a random walk with no drift. The market maker posts a bid below the mid and an ask above it, accumulating inventory as trades arrive. The model produces a reservation price equal to the mid-price reduced by a term proportional to current inventory, risk aversion, price variance, and time remaining. When inventory is long, the reservation price falls below the mid, naturally setting lower quotes that attract buyers and discourage sellers. The optimal total spread has two parts: a component that compensates for inventory risk and a component that captures the profit margin from providing liquidity. [1]

deepDive.heading: Market Making Strategies: Delta-Neutral vs Directional
deepDive.body: Delta-neutral market makers hedge accumulated inventory against correlated instruments such as index futures or ETFs, aiming to earn the bid-ask spread with no net directional exposure. Hedging costs reduce spread income but limit downside from adverse price moves. Directional market makers tilt their quotes based on short-term order flow signals, taking on measured positions when signals are strong. In practice most HFT market makers target delta neutrality at the portfolio level but carry small tactical positions when signals warrant. Risk limits on inventory per security, total portfolio delta, and overnight positions enforce discipline. [1]

deepDive.heading: Market Maker Obligations and Designated Market Makers
deepDive.body: Exchange-designated market makers (DMMs) have formal obligations in exchange for privileges. NYSE DMM obligations: maintain continuous two-sided quotes, facilitate price discovery at the open and close (by accepting imbalances), step in during unusual volatility. DMM privileges: access to co-location, order flow information, ability to see incoming orders before they execute (flash orders — now banned). NASDAQ Market Makers: less formal obligations; compete with other market makers and HFT firms. Options market makers: required to quote for all strikes and expirations in their assigned underlyings, within maximum bid-ask spread width requirements. The regulatory framework attempts to ensure that market-making benefits (tight spreads, continuous liquidity) are available even in less liquid securities where pure HFT market making wouldn't be profitable. [2]

takeaways: Market makers earn the bid-ask spread in exchange for providing continuous liquidity and absorbing inventory risk. [1]
Avellaneda-Stoikov: optimal quotes are a function of current inventory, time horizon, risk aversion, and volatility. [1]
Reservation price: mid-price adjusted downward when long inventory — attracts buying to reduce the position. [1]
Adverse selection: market makers widen spreads / cancel quotes when they detect informed order flow. [1]
HFT market makers are typically delta-neutral (hedge inventory with ETFs/futures) and flat overnight. [2]

edgeCases.q: How do market makers handle liquidity crises when they need to reduce inventory fast?
edgeCases.a: In normal conditions, market makers manage inventory gradually through quote skewing. In stress conditions (sudden large adverse price move, news event, circuit breaker threat), they may need to exit immediately. Options: (1) Aggressive market orders — place market sell orders to exit long inventory regardless of price; accept significant market impact; (2) Cross-venue hedging — sell correlated instruments (ETF, futures) on faster/more liquid venues first while trying to unwind the primary position; (3) Risk limit breach → mandatory flat — when inventory or P&L loss crosses pre-set limits, an automated system places market orders to flatten the book entirely; (4) Widen spreads / pull quotes — stop providing liquidity to stop inventory from growing worse; this is the 'liquidity withdrawal' that amplifies volatility during crashes. The 2010 Flash Crash occurred partly because HFT market makers used option (4), creating a vacuum. Post-crash, circuit breakers and stub quote elimination were introduced to prevent the worst outcomes.

edgeCases.q: How does market making in options differ from equity market making?
edgeCases.a: Options market making is fundamentally different because of non-linear payoffs (the Greeks). An equity market maker primarily manages delta (directional inventory risk). An options market maker manages all Greeks: delta (directional exposure — hedge with underlying shares/futures), gamma (convexity — large moves cause accelerating P&L swings; long gamma is profitable but expensive), vega (volatility exposure — options prices depend on implied volatility; must hedge vega across the vol surface), theta (time decay — market makers are typically short gamma/vega and earn theta decay over time), rho (interest rate sensitivity). The options market maker's key insight: they're primarily trading implied volatility (IV) against their model of fair value volatility. If IV is high vs. their forecast, they sell options (short vega); if IV is low, they buy. Hedging the delta, gamma, and vega simultaneously while managing the bid-ask spread across hundreds of strikes and expirations is an extremely complex optimisation problem.

sources.label: Avellaneda, M. & Stoikov, S. (2008) — High-Frequency Trading in a Limit Order Book, Quantitative Finance
Harris, L. — Trading and Exchanges: Market Microstructure for Practitioners, Oxford University Press (2002)
